title: Building Verb Intuition
summary: Showcases how to formulate intuitive phrasing based on verb valency and form.
text: Transitive Verbs Form Verb Intuitive Phrase Example Plain 食べる someone eats something 子どもがりんごを食べる。 The child eats an apple. 褒める someone praises someone 先生が学生を褒める。 The teacher praises the student. 頼む someone asks someone for something 私が友達に助けを頼む。 I ask my friend for help. Negative 食べる someone does not eat something 子どもが野菜を食べない。 The child does not eat vegetables. 褒める someone does not praise someone 先生がその学生を褒めない。 The teacher does not praise that student. 頼む someone does not ask someone for something 私は彼に何も頼まない。 I do not ask him for anything. Past 食べる someone ate something 子どもがりんごを食べた。 The child ate an apple. 褒める someone praised someone 先生が学生を褒めた。 The teacher praised the student. 頼む someone asked someone for something 私が友達に助けを頼んだ。 I asked my friend for help. Passive 食べる something is eaten by someone りんごが子どもに食べられた。 The apple was eaten by the child. 褒める someone is praised by someone 学生が先生に褒められた。 The student was praised by the teacher. 頼む someone is asked for something by someone 私が友達に手伝いを頼まれた。 My friend asked me for help. Causative 食べる someone makes someone eat something 母が子どもに野菜を食べさせる。 The mother makes the child eat vegetables. 褒める someone makes someone praise someone 校長が先生に学生を褒めさせる。 The principal makes the teacher praise the student. 頼む someone makes someone ask someone for something 上司が私に部長に許可を頼ませる。 My boss makes me ask the department head for permission. Causative-passive 食べる someone is made to eat something 子どもが母に野菜を食べさせられる。 The child is made by the mother to eat vegetables. 褒める someone is made to praise someone 先生が校長に学生を褒めさせられる。 The teacher is made by the principal to praise the student. 頼む someone is made to ask someone for something 私が上司に部長に許可を頼ませられる。 I am made by my boss to ask the department head for permission. Potential 食べる someone can eat something 子どもはそのりんごを食べられる。 The child can eat that apple. 褒める someone can praise someone 先生はその学生を褒められる。 The teacher can praise that student. 頼む someone can ask someone for something 私は彼に協力を頼める。 I can ask him for his cooperation. Volitional 食べる someone will eat something 私はこのケーキを食べよう。 I'll eat this cake. 褒める someone will praise someone 先生は次もその学生を褒めよう。 The teacher will praise that student again next time. 頼む someone will ask someone for something 私は先生に推薦状を頼もう。 I'll ask the teacher for a letter of recommendation. たい 食べる someone wants to eat something 私はそのケーキを食べたい。 I want to eat that cake. 褒める someone wants to praise someone 私は彼を褒めたい。 I want to praise him. 頼む someone wants to ask someone for something 私は友達に助けを頼みたい。 I want to ask my friend for help. ている 食べる someone is eating something 彼は朝ご飯を食べている。 He is eating breakfast. 褒める someone is praising someone 先生がみんなの前で学生を褒めている。 The teacher is praising the student in front of everyone. 頼む someone is asking someone for something 彼は今、友達に助けを頼んでいる。 He's asking his friend for help right now. てしまう 食べる someone ends up eating something 子どもがお菓子を全部食べてしまった。 The child ended up eating all the snacks. 褒める someone ends up praising someone 先生は思わず彼を褒めてしまった。 The teacher ended up praising him without meaning to. 頼む someone ends up asking someone for something 私はつい彼にお金を頼んでしまった。 I ended up asking him for money. Intransitive Verbs Form Verb Intuitive Phrase Example Plain 行く someone goes 彼が学校に行く。 He goes to school. 起きる someone wakes up 彼が六時に起きる。 He wakes up at six. 座る someone sits 彼がいすに座る。 He sits in a chair. Negative 行く someone does not go 彼は学校に行かない。 He does not go to school. 起きる someone does not wake up 彼はなかなか起きない。 He does not wake up easily. 座る someone does not sit 彼はいすに座らない。 He does not sit in the chair. Past 行く someone went 彼が学校に行った。 He went to school. 起きる someone woke up 彼が六時に起きた。 He woke up at six. 座る someone sat 彼がいすに座った。 He sat in a chair. Passive 行く someone is affected by someone going 私は友達に先に行かれた。 My friend went on ahead of me, leaving me behind. 起きる someone is affected by someone waking up 私は子どもに早く起きられた。 The child woke up early, and it affected me. 座る someone is affected by someone sitting 私は隣の人に近くに座られた。 The person next to me sat too close to me. Causative 行く someone makes someone go 母が子どもを学校に行かせる。 The mother makes the child go to school. 起きる someone makes someone wake up 母が子どもを早く起きさせる。 The mother makes the child wake up early. 座る someone makes someone sit 先生が学生をいすに座らせる。 The teacher makes the student sit in a chair. Causative-passive 行く someone is made to go 子どもが母に学校に行かせられる。 The child is made by the mother to go to school. 起きる someone is made to wake up 子どもが母に早く起きさせられる。 The child is made by the mother to wake up early. 座る someone is made to sit 学生が先生にいすに座らせられる。 The student is made by the teacher to sit in a chair. Potential 行く someone can go 私は明日学校に行ける。 I can go to school tomorrow. 起きる someone can wake up 彼は六時に起きられる。 He can wake up at six. 座る someone can sit 私はそこに座れる。 I can sit there. Volitional 行く someone will go 私は明日そこに行こう。 I'll go there tomorrow. 起きる someone will wake up 明日は六時に起きよう。 I'll wake up at six tomorrow. 座る someone will sit 私はここに座ろう。 I'll sit here. たい 行く someone wants to go 私は日本に行きたい。 I want to go to Japan. 起きる someone wants to wake up 明日は早く起きたい。 I want to wake up early tomorrow. 座る someone wants to sit 私はそこに座りたい。 I want to sit there. ている 行く someone is going 彼は今、学校に行っている。 He's on his way to school now. 起きる someone is awake 彼はもう起きている。 He's already awake. 座る someone is sitting 彼は窓のそばに座っている。 He is sitting by the window. てしまう 行く someone ends up going 彼は一人で行ってしまった。 He ended up going by himself. 起きる someone ends up waking up 彼は朝早く起きてしまった。 He ended up waking up early in the morning. 座る someone ends up sitting 私は後ろの席に座ってしまった。 I ended up sitting in the back.

title: Copula Conjugations
summary: Reference tables for plain, polite, honorific, and written Japanese copula forms.
text: Basic Conjugations Form Plain Polite Honorific Non-past だ です でございます Past だった でした でございました Non-past negative ではない じゃない ではありません ではないです じゃありません じゃないです ではございません Past negative ではなかった じゃなかった ではありませんでした じゃありませんでした ではございませんでした Volitional だろう でしょう でございましょう Continuative (連用形) で でありまして でございまして Conditional なら ならば であれば であれば The polite negative column includes both the more formal では forms and the conversational じゃ variants that often appear in everyday speech. Written Explanatory Forms In writing, the following forms may appear when the author is explaining or presenting something in a more formal expository register. Form Plain Polite Non-past である であります Past であった でありました Plain Form Examples The table below gives representative plain-style examples for the core forms above, including both the standard では negatives and the conversational じゃ variants. Function Japanese English Non-past (affirmative) 彼は学生だ。 He is a student. Non-past (negative) 彼は学生ではない。 He is not a student. Non-past (negative, casual) 彼は学生じゃない。 He is not a student. Past (affirmative) 彼は学生だった。 He was a student. Past (negative) 彼は学生ではなかった。 He was not a student. Past (negative, casual) 彼は学生じゃなかった。 He was not a student. Volitional / conjectural 彼は学生だろう。 He is probably a student. Continuative (連用形) 彼は学生で、彼女は先生だ。 He is a student, and she is a teacher. Conditional (なら) 彼が学生なら、この本を知っているはずだ。 If he's a student, he should know this book. Conditional (ならば) 彼が学生ならば、図書館にいるかもしれない。 If he's a student, he might be in the library. Notes で is the continuative (連用形) form of the copula and can connect clauses, including weak causal links depending on context. なら and ならば behave as conditional forms in plain style, while であれば serves the more formal parallel.

title: Verb Conjugation
summary: Two-sided reference tables for godan, ichidan, irregular, and て-form / た-form verb conjugations.
text: Core Conjugations 五段 Note 五段 Form 一段 一段 Note 書く ← Dictionary → 食べる あ-row 書か ない ← Negative → 食べ ない drop る あ-row 書か れる ← Passive → 食べ られる drop る あ-row 書か せる ← Causative → 食べ させる drop る あ-row 書か せられる ・書か される ← Causative-Passive → 食べ させられる drop る い-row 書き ます ← Polite → 食べ ます drop る う-row 書く な ← Prohibitive → 食べる な drop る え-row 書け る ← Potential → 食べ られる ・食べ れる drop る え-row 書け ば ← Conditional → 食べ れば drop る え-row 書け ← Imperative → 食べ ろ ・食べ よ drop る お-row 書こ う ← Volitional → 食べ よう drop る (See below) 書 いて ← て-Form → 食べ て drop る (See below) 書 いた ← Past → 食べ た drop る Irregular Verbs する Form くる しない ← Negative → こない される ← Passive → こられる させる ← Causative → こさせる させられる ← Causative-Passive → こさせられる します ← Polite → きます するな ← Prohibitive → くるな できる ← Potential → こられる すれば ← Conditional → くれば しろ・せよ ← Imperative → こい しよう ← Volitional → こよう して ← て-Form → きて した ← Past → きた て-Form (+ た-Form) Conjugation Final Kana / Verb て-Form Suffix た-Form Suffix う・つ・る って った む・ぶ・ぬ んで んだ く いて いた ぐ いで いだ す して した Special cases くる きて きた する して した 行く 行って 行った Notes The ichidan potential form 食べられる has the same surface form as the passive, so context determines which meaning is intended. The same overlap appears with こられる , which can be either the potential or passive form of くる . Godan verbs that end in う use わ (instead of あ ) for negative form ( 買う → 買わない ).

title: Casual Reductions
summary: A compact reference for common casual Japanese reductions and compressed spoken forms, showing what fuller Japanese they map to and how to interpret them while reading.
text: Surface form Natural Japanese Meaning Examples 〜てる 〜ている be doing; be in a state; ongoing action or resulting state 何してる？ What are you doing? ドアが開いてる。 The door is open. 〜てた 〜ていた was doing; was in a state さっき寝てた。 I was sleeping earlier. ずっと黙ってた。 He stayed quiet the whole time. 〜ちゃう 〜てしまう end up doing; do completely; do unintentionally; may carry regret, disappointment, or “unfortunately” feeling 忘れちゃうよ。 I’ll end up forgetting. そんなこと言っちゃうの？ Are you really going to say something like that? 〜ちゃった 〜てしまった ended up doing; did completely; did unintentionally; often carries regret, “oops,” or unfortunate-result feeling 忘れちゃった。 I forgot. ケーキ全部食べちゃった。 I ended up eating the whole cake. 〜とく 〜ておく do in advance; do in preparation; leave as is for now メモしとく。 I’ll make a note of it. そのままにしといて。 Leave it as it is. 〜といた 〜ておいた did in advance; did in preparation; left as is intentionally もう言っといた。 I already told them. 席、取っといたよ。 I saved you a seat. 〜なきゃ 〜なければならない / 〜なければいけない must do; have to do 行かなきゃ。 I have to go. もう寝なきゃ。 I have to go to sleep now. 〜なくちゃ 〜なくてはならない / 〜なくてはいけない must do; have to do 勉強しなくちゃ。 I have to study. 急がなくちゃ。 I have to hurry. 〜んだ 〜のだ explanatory; gives background, reason, or clarification; “the thing is ...” 今日は休みなんだ。 The thing is, I’m off today. 行きたくないんだ。 It’s that I don’t want to go. 〜んです 〜のです explanatory; gives background, reason, or clarification politely ちょっと忙しいんです。 It’s just that I’m a bit busy. 頭が痛いんです。 It’s that I have a headache. って と / という / というのは / とは quotation marker; “that”; “called”; can also mark hearsay or topic reframing depending on context 明日行くって。 They said they’re going tomorrow. パンダってかわいいよね。 Pandas are cute, aren’t they? 〜っけ 〜だっけ / 〜たっけ what was it again?; used when trying to recall something 明日だっけ？ Was it tomorrow again? 名前なんだっけ？ What was their name again? 〜じゃん 〜じゃないか isn’t it?; see?; it is, after all; points something out as obvious or now apparent いいじゃん。 It’s fine, isn’t it? できるじゃん。 See? You can do it. 〜だろ 〜だろう probably; I expect; right? 来るだろ。 He’ll probably come. わかるだろ？ You get it, right? 〜でしょ 〜でしょう probably; right?; surely そうでしょ？ Right? 明日は暑いでしょ。 It’ll probably be hot tomorrow. 〜ねえ / 〜ねぇ 〜ない not 知らねえ。 I don’t know. そんな暇ねえよ。 I don’t have that kind of time. わかんない わからない don’t understand; can’t tell これ、わかんない。 I don’t understand this. どっちがいいかわかんない。 I can’t tell which one is better. しんない / 知んない 知らない don’t know そんなのしんないよ。 I don’t know about that. あの人のこと、よくしんない。 I don’t really know that person. そりゃ それは that is; well, of course; given that そりゃそうだ。 Well, of course. そりゃ怒るよ。 Well, of course he’d get mad. こんなん こんなの / こんなもの this kind of thing; something like this こんなん初めてだ。 This kind of thing is a first for me. こんなんでいいの？ Is something like this really okay? そんなん そんなの / そんなもの that kind of thing; something like that そんなん無理だよ。 That kind of thing is impossible. そんなん知らない。 I don’t know anything about that kind of thing. すげえ すごい amazing; intense; awful/a lot, depending on context すげえな。 That’s amazing. 今日、風すげえ。 The wind is really strong today. やべえ やばい bad; dangerous; awful; amazing; “oh no,” depending on context やべえ、遅れる。 Oh no, I’m going to be late. この店、やべえくらいうまい。 This place is insanely good. つーか というか or rather; I mean; more like 変というか、変わってる。 Weird, or rather, unusual. 嫌いっていうか、苦手。 It’s not so much that I dislike it; I’m just bad with it. 〜てくれ 〜てください please do; do this for me ちょっと待ってくれ。 Wait a second. もうやめてくれ。 Please stop already.

title: V1てV2 — How V2 Shapes Meaning
summary: How each verb in the V2 slot shapes the meaning of the action expressed by V1.
text: V2 verb Precise meaning relative to X (V1) Examples Core て-form follow-ons みる Try X; do X and see what happens Implies tentative attempt, not visual seeing 新しいアプリを使ってみた。 I tried using a new app. 少し考えてみます。 I'll try thinking it over a bit. 食べてみてください。 Please give it a try. おく Do X in advance; leave things in a state where X is already handled Forward-looking preparation; the effect persists 先に予約しておいた。 I reserved it in advance. 必要なところをメモしておく。 I'll jot down the important parts for later. ドアを開けておいて。 Leave the door open. しまう X ends up happening; often regrettably, accidentally, or beyond one's control. It can also simply mean "finish X completely." 財布をなくしてしまった。 I ended up losing my wallet. 宿題をやってしまった。 I got the homework out of the way. 食べてしまった… I ended up eating it all... いく X proceeds away from the present point onward; change or development continues into the future Direction: now → future 日本語が分かっていく。 I'll gradually come to understand Japanese. 気温が下がっていく。 The temperature keeps dropping. 社会は変わっていくだろう。 Society will keep changing. くる X comes about toward the present point; a change has been developing and has now arrived here Direction: past → now (contrast with いく) 雨が降ってきた。 It's started raining. 日本語が分かってきた。 I'm starting to understand Japanese. 疲れてきた。 I've been getting tired. ある X has been done intentionally and the resulting state persists; the effect was deliberately set up Usually with transitive verbs; stresses an intentional prepared result 会場はきれいに片づけてある。 The venue has been tidied up and is ready. ドアが閉めてある。 The door has been left closed on purpose. 必要な情報はもう書いてある。 The necessary information has already been written down. いる X is ongoing right now, or the state resulting from X holds currently Neutral about intent; often either ongoing action or current state 彼は座っている。 He's sitting. 窓が開いている。 The window is open. 雨が降っている。 It's raining. Giving & receiving verbs (やりもらい) あげる Do X for someone else (speaker or in-group → other); a favor directed outward Not used when the recipient is the speaker 彼に教えてあげた。 I taught him as a favor. 荷物を持ってあげる。 I'll carry the bags for her. 子供に読んであげた。 I read to the child. くれる Someone does X for me, us, or my in-group; a favor directed inward toward the speaker Subject is someone outside the speaker's side 友達が教えてくれた。 A friend explained it to me. 母がケーキを作ってくれた。 My mother made me a cake. 先生が助けてくれた。 The teacher helped me out. くださる Someone socially above the speaker does X for me or us; honorific form of くれる Use toward superiors, teachers, customers, etc. 先生が説明してくださいました。 The teacher kindly explained it to me. 部長が手伝ってくださいました。 The manager kindly helped me. 先輩が資料を送ってくださいました。 My senior kindly sent me the materials. もらう Receive the benefit of someone doing X; have someone do X from the speaker's receiving perspective 友達に手伝ってもらった。 I had a friend help me. 先生に説明してもらう。 I'll have the teacher explain it. 医者に診てもらった。 I had the doctor examine me. Volition & desire みせる Do X demonstratively to prove oneself; strong personal resolve expressed through action "I'll show you (that I can do it)" 絶対に勝ってみせる。 I'll definitely win; just watch me. やり遂げてみせる。 I'll see it through, no matter what. 今度は一人でやってみせる。 Next time I'll do it by myself and prove it. ほしい Want someone else to do X; expresses the speaker's desire for another person's action Subject of X must be someone other than the speaker もっと話してほしい。 I want you to talk more. 早く来てほしかった。 I wanted you to come sooner. 彼に謝ってほしい。 I want him to apologize.

title: ます Stem Compound Verbs
summary: Shows how common V2 verbs shape the meaning of compounds built from the ます stem.
text: V2 verb Precise meaning relative to X (V1) Examples Phase / progression compounds はじめる Begin X; neutral start of an action More neutral than ～だす, which often feels abrupt 本を読み始めた。 I started reading the book. 急に話し始めた。 They suddenly started talking. 仕事を探し始める。 I'll start looking for work. だす X suddenly begins; an action bursts into motion, often unexpectedly Abrupt onset; more sudden than ～はじめる 子供が急に泣き出した。 The child suddenly burst into tears. 雨が降り出した。 It suddenly started raining. 彼女は走り出した。 She broke into a run. つづける Continue X over time; keep doing X 三時間話し続けた。 They kept talking for three hours. 彼は同じ会社で働き続けている。 He has continued working at the same company. 勉強し続けるのは大変だ。 It is hard to keep studying. おわる Finish X; an action reaches its natural end point レポートを書き終わった。 I finished writing the report. 食べ終わったら教えて。 Let me know when you're done eating. 映画を見終わった。 I finished watching the movie. きる Carry X through completely; do X to the very end or to its limit Often implies full completion despite effort or difficulty 長い小説を読み切った。 I read the long novel all the way through. 一万円を使い切ってしまった。 I used up the entire 10,000 yen. 最後までやり切ろう。 Let's see it through to the very end. かける X is left incomplete: X started, nearly happened, or was about to happen, but did not fully reach completion. Think "partway / almost / not all the way through". 書きかけた手紙を机の上に置いた。 I left the half-written letter on the desk. 言いかけてやめた。 I started to say it, then stopped. 食べかけのパンがある。 There's a half-eaten piece of bread. Redo / response / interaction なおす Do X again in order to fix, correct, or improve it 作文を書き直した。 I rewrote the composition. もう一度考え直してください。 Please think it over again. 計画を作り直す必要がある。 We need to redo the plan. かえす Do X back in return; respond with the same kind of action toward the other side Common in retaliation, comeback, or return-action meanings ひどいことを言われて、言い返した。 They said something awful, so I answered back. 相手にやり返すつもりだ。 I intend to retaliate against them. 殴られても殴り返すな。 Even if you get hit, don't hit back. あう Do X mutually or together; parties engage in X with each other 二人で助け合って生きてきた。 The two of them have lived by helping each other. その問題について話し合った。 We discussed that issue together. 最後まで競い合った。 They competed with each other until the end. Failure / missed completion そこなう・そこねる Miss the chance to do X; fail to do X properly or successfully before completion 言い損なってしまった。 I missed my chance to say it. 電車に乗り損なった。 I failed to catch the train. 大事なところを聞き損ねた。 I missed an important part of what was said.

title: Common Japanese Particles
summary: Provides a breakdown of how most Japanese particles work.
text: Particle First parse while reading Also check for Key contrast / note Examples が the noun most directly tied to the predicate; often the subject with perception/psychology predicates, may mark the thing perceived, understood, liked, etc. rather than the experiencer Do not confuse with は. が identifies the noun the predicate applies to most directly; は sets the topic/contrast frame. 猫がいる there is a cat 雨が降る rain falls 日本語が分かる understand Japanese 猫が好きだ like cats を direct object; the thing directly affected by the action with movement/traversal predicates, may mark the path crossed or the place exited When the verb suggests motion, ask whether を is really an object. ご飯を食べる eat rice 本を読む read a book 公園を歩く walk through the park 家を出る leave the house に destination, target, recipient, time, location of existence with change-of-state predicates, may mark the resulting state A useful contrast: に often marks where something ends up / is aimed / exists. 学校に行く go to school 友だちに会う meet a friend 七時に起きる wake up at 7 机の上に本がある there is a book on the desk 医者になる become a doctor へ direction toward Usually similar to に with motion, but more “toward” than “arrive at.” 東京へ行く go to Tokyo / head to Tokyo 南へ向かう head south で 1. location where an action happens 2. means / instrument 3. state / condition / manner 4. conjunctive だ・です Contrast 1 with に : で marks where an action is carried out; に marks where something exists / ends up. Contrast 3 with な -adjective + に : に makes an adverbial modifier directly, while で frames the subject as being “in that state” while doing the action. 1. 駅で待つ wait at the station 2. バスで行く go by bus ナイフで切る cut with a knife 3. 必死で逃げる flee in desperation / flee frantically Compare: 必死に逃げる flee desperately; the fleeing itself is described as desperate 4. 学生で、先生でもある is a student and also a teacher と with; and; quotation; counterpart as a clause connector meaning “when / if”; for noun-listing contrasts such as と / や / とか〜とか / 〜も〜も, see Listing Markers and Clause Linkers と lists exhaustively; や is non-exhaustive. It also marks quoted words/thoughts. 友だちと行く go with a friend 本とノート books and notebooks 「大丈夫」と言った said “It’s okay” から from; starting point; source reason / cause (“because”) Often pairs with まで for “from X to Y.” 東京から来た came from Tokyo 九時から始まる starts at 9 寒いから帰る go home because it’s cold まで until; as far as; endpoint / limit Often pairs with から. 金曜日まで働く work until Friday 駅まで歩く walk as far as the station より than; comparison basis source in formal/literary contexts In everyday comparison, it often appears with ほうが. 電車より速い faster than the train 昨日より忙しい busier than yesterday も also; too can replace a case particle or stack after one Read it first as “also / too,” then check what it is attaching to. 私も行く I’m going too 猫も好きだ I also like cats 家にもある there’s one at home too は topic; contrast は is not the same as が. It frames what the sentence is about, and often implies contrast even if subtle. 私は学生だ as for me, I’m a student 魚は食べる I eat fish / fish, I do eat の possession; attribution; noun-to-noun linking clause nominalization in some contexts Read it first as “X modifies Y.” Sometimes that is possession, sometimes category/attribute. 先生の本 the teacher’s book 日本の会社 a Japanese company 赤いのが欲しい I want the red one 食べるのが好きだ I like eating や non-exhaustive listing: “X, Y, and such” compare with と, とか〜とか, and 〜も〜も in Listing Markers Unlike と, it suggests other items are not being listed. 本や雑誌 books, magazines, and such りんごやバナナを買う buy apples, bananas, and so on か question marker; alternative embedded question / uncertainty At sentence end, it often marks a question. Inside a clause, it often means “whether / which / where / etc.” 行きますか are you going? コーヒーかお茶 coffee or tea どこへ行くか分からない I don’t know where [someone] is going

title: Conversions
summary: Common Japanese conversion patterns between nouns, adjectives, adverbs, and verbs.
text: Noun → Adjective Pattern How it works Examples Noun + 的 Forms a な-adjective from a subset of pure nouns. This pattern is more common with abstract concepts and has tighter lexical limits than + の . 文化 → 文化的 culture → cultural 歴史 → 歴史的 history → historical 科学 → 科学的 science → scientific Noun + の Works productively with pure nouns. Grammatically this is an adjectival noun phrase rather than a true adjective, but it behaves like an adjective in practice. 車の本 a book about cars 木の椅子 a wooden chair Warning: Noun + 的 is not freely available for every noun, so it is safer to learn it as a lexical pattern rather than assuming it can always be formed. Adjective → Adverb Pattern How it works Examples い-adjective stem + く Removes the final い and turns the adjective into an adverbial form. 私は京都に長く住んでいました。 I lived in Kyoto for a long time. そのラーメンを早く食べました。 I ate that ramen quickly. な-adjective stem + に Uses the adjective stem and adds に . もっと効率的に日本語が勉強したいです。 I want to study Japanese more efficiently. 図書館に雑誌を静かに読んでいることがよくする。 I often read magazines quietly in the library. Adjective → Noun Pattern Quality type Examples い-adjective stem + さ Objective or measurable quality. 高い → 高さ tall / expensive → tallness / expensiveness な-adjective stem + さ Objective or measurable quality. 便利な → 便利さ convenient → convenience い-adjective stem + み Subjective, experiential, or felt quality. 面白い → 面白み interesting → interest / interestingness な-adjective stem + み Subjective quality, but only for a small set of adjectives. 新鮮な → 新鮮み fresh → freshness Warning: + み is much more lexically restricted than + さ , especially with な-adjectives. Verb → Noun Pattern How it works Examples Plain verb + の Nominalizes the verb phrase. In practice it behaves like a noun phrase. 甘い物を食べるのが好きだ。 I like eating sweet things. Plain verb + こと Another nominalizer, often slightly more formal or abstract than の . 全部の漢字を暗記する事ができないかな。 I wonder if I can memorize all the kanji. Verb ます-stem Some verbs have lexicalized noun uses in the ます-stem. This is not productive across all verbs. 富士山は年をとるの登り難しくなった。 As I get older, it has become more difficult to climb Mt. Fuji. 今日ここから富士山は見やすいです。 It's easy to see Mt. Fuji from here today. Warning: Stem-based noun readings are lexical items, not something you can safely generate for every verb.

title: Dictionary Structure Markers
summary: Reference table for navigating common J-J dictionary structure markers.
text: Abbrev Full Form Reading Meaning What it tells you 名 名詞 めいし noun behaves as thing/entity 動 動詞 どうし verb action/state predicate 形 形容詞 けいようし i-adjective ends in ～い, inflects 形動 形容動詞 けいようどうし na-adjective needs だ/な 副 副詞 ふくし adverb modifies verbs/adjectives 五 五段 ごだん godan verb conjugation pattern 一 一段 いちだん ichidan verb simpler conjugation サ変 サ行変格 さぎょうへんかく irregular (する) special verb class 話 話し言葉 はなしことば spoken casual / conversational 書 書き言葉 かきことば written formal / literary

title: Additive Linkers
summary: Compares clause and predicate additive linkers such as 〜て, 〜し, 〜で, and 〜くて.
text: Form Connects Scenario Examples Notes 〜て Clause (verb) Sequence 朝ご飯を食べて、学校に行く。 I eat breakfast and then go to school. Default clause linker. Interpreted as sequence or simple addition depending on context. 〜て Clause (verb) Cause/result (soft) 雨が降って、試合が中止になった。 It rained, and the game was canceled. Can imply causality, but this is a tendency, not encoded explicitly. 〜て Clause (verb) State chaining ドアが開いて、風が入ってきた。 The door opened, and the wind came in. Often links events that feel naturally connected in time or situation. 〜て Clause (verb) Ongoing -> result 音楽を聞いていて、寝てしまった。 I was listening to music and fell asleep. First clause often provides background state. 〜し Clause (predicate) Listing qualities 彼は優しいし、頭もいい。 He's kind and smart. Enumerates separate points. Each clause stands independently. 〜し Clause (predicate) Multiple reasons 雨だし、時間もないし、行かない。 It's raining and I don't have time, so I won't go. Commonly used for justification; can imply "so." 〜し Clause (predicate) Open-ended listing 安いし、おいしい。 It's cheap and tasty (among other things). Often implies additional unstated reasons. 〜し Clause (predicate) Additive (written) 次の表現を勉強し、文も作る。 Study the following expressions and write some sentences too. In written style, can resemble 〜て but still retains a "listing" feel. 〜で Noun / な-adj State linking 彼は学生で、アルバイトもしている。 He's a student, and he also works part-time. Links states, roles, or descriptions related to the same subject. 〜で Noun / な-adj Reason / cause 病気で、学校を休んだ。 I missed school because I was sick. Often gives a straightforward reason, especially with conditions, situations, or circumstances. 〜くて い-adj State linking 安くておいしい。 It's cheap and delicious. Links qualities into a combined description. 〜くて い-adj Reason / cause 頭が痛くて、寝た。 I went to bed because my head hurt. Often gives a natural reason, especially with feelings, physical states, or conditions.

title: Clause Linkers
summary: Compares major Japanese clause linkers such as から, ので, けど, が, のに, と, たら, ば, なら, and ても / でも.
text: Connector Core Meaning Read Clause 1 As... Typical Nuance Examples から because / since the direct reason for what follows direct, speaker-assertive reason 雨が降ったから、試合は中止になった。 Because it rained, the game was canceled. 頭が痛いから、早く帰ります。 Because I have a headache, I'll go home early. ので because / since the explanatory background reason softer, more neutral, less blunt than から 電車が遅れたので、遅刻しました。 Because the train was late, I got there late. 危ないので、入らないでください。 Because it's dangerous, please don't go in. けど but / though a fact that contrasts with or limits what follows common in speech; often softens what comes next 行きたいけど、時間がない。 I want to go, but I don't have time. この店は高いけど、おいしい。 This restaurant is expensive, but it's delicious. が but / though a fact that contrasts with what follows, or a polite lead-in to what follows contrast in statements; often a polite transition in requests or softening expressions 行きたいですが、今日は無理です。 I want to go, but it won't work today. すみませんが、もう一度お願いします。 Excuse me, but could you do that one more time? のに even though / despite that background that should have led to a different result contrary expectation; often surprise, frustration, or complaint 勉強したのに、失敗した。 Even though I studied, I failed. もう春なのに、まだ寒い。 Even though it's already spring, it's still cold. と when / if the trigger that automatically leads to the next clause automatic, regular, or natural consequence; usually not used when clause 2 is a decision, intention, request, invitation, or command 春になると、花が咲く。 When spring comes, flowers bloom. このボタンを押すと、ドアが開きます。 When you press this button, the door opens. たら if / when / after the event that must happen first once X happens, then Y; event-first and very flexible in real use 雨がやんだら、出かけます。 If the rain stops, I'll go out. 家に帰ったら、電話してください。 When you get home, please call me. ば if the condition under which clause 2 holds conditional or hypothetical relationship; often less tied to a concrete event sequence than たら 時間があれば、行きます。 If I have time, I'll go. 安ければ、買います。 If it's cheap, I'll buy it. なら if / given that the assumption, topic, or case now being considered reactive, assumption-based, often used when responding to what has just been said or when narrowing to a specific case 日本に行くなら、京都にも行きたい。 If I'm going to Japan, I want to go to Kyoto too. 時間がないなら、今日はやめましょう。 If we don't have time, let's stop for today. ても / でも even if / even though a condition that still does not change the result concessive: clause 2 holds even if clause 1 is true 雨が降っても、行きます。 Even if it rains, I'll go. 高くても、必要なら買います。 Even if it's expensive, I'll buy it if I need it.

title: Clauses & Predicates
summary: Provides an overview of how these easily confused grammar terms function with respect to Japanese sentences.
text: Definitions Predicate The word (or expression), including all attached auxiliaries, that carries tense, polarity (positive / negative), or asserts a state and can end a sentence when in its final form. Predicate Types Verbs い-adjectives Nouns + (だ・です) <- copula optional Clause A predicate together with its dependents. Examples 朝ご飯を食べて 、 学校に行く 。 彼は優しいし 、 頭もいい 。 次の表現を勉強し 、 文も作る 。 音楽を聞いていて 、 寝てしまった 。 雨だし 、 時間もないし 、 行かない 。 彼は学生で 、 兄は先生 。 Finite Predicate A predicate in the form that actually ends a sentence and carries tense / polarity for the whole sentence. Examples 朝ご飯を食べて、学校に 行く 。 彼は優しいし、頭も いい 。 次の表現を勉強し、文も 作る 。 音楽を聞いていて、 寝てしまった 。 雨だし、時間もないし、 行かない 。 彼は学生で、兄は 先生 。 Non-Finite Predicate A predicate in the form that is shaped to connect to or modify what follows. Examples 朝ご飯を 食べて 、学校に行く。 彼は 優しいし 、頭もいい。 次の表現を 勉強し 、文も作る。 音楽を 聞いていて 、寝てしまった。 雨 だし 、時間も ないし 、行かない。 彼は 学生で 、兄は先生。

title: Listing Markers
summary: Compares Japanese listing markers such as と, や, とか〜とか, and 〜も〜も.
text: Form Connects Scenario Examples Notes 〜と Noun Exhaustive listing パンと卵を買った。 I bought bread and eggs. Presents the listed items as a closed set. 〜や Noun Non-exhaustive listing パンや卵を買った。 I bought things like bread and eggs. Gives representative examples rather than a complete list. 〜とか〜とか Noun / phrase Casual rough listing 休みの日は、映画とかゲームとかをする。 On my days off, I do things like watch movies and play games. Casual and loose. Often used in speech to give rough examples. 〜も〜も Noun Both A and B コーヒーも紅茶も飲む。 I drink both coffee and tea. Emphasizes inclusion of all listed items.

title: Sentence Connectors
summary: Compares sentence-level Japanese connectors such as そして, それから, それに, だから, でも, and しかし.
text: Connector Core Meaning Use It For... Typical Nuance Examples Additive / sequence そして and; and then adding the next event or point in a neutral written/spoken style simple progression or addition; not the normal choice for cause/result 朝ご飯を食べた。そして、学校に行った。 I ate breakfast. And then I went to school. この本は分かりやすい。そして、例文も多い。 This book is easy to understand. It also has a lot of example sentences. それから after that; and then moving step by step through a sequence of events temporal progression is especially clear 宿題をした。それから、映画を見た。 I did my homework. After that, I watched a movie. 駅で友だちに会った。それから、一緒に昼ご飯を食べた。 I met my friend at the station. After that, we ate lunch together. それに besides; moreover adding another supporting reason or point often feels like “and on top of that” この店は安い。それに、おいしい。 This restaurant is cheap. On top of that, it's tasty. 彼は親切だ。それに、説明も上手だ。 He's kind. On top of that, he's also good at explaining things. また also; additionally adding another point in neutral or formal explanation common in writing, presentations, and explanations この文法は会話でよく使う。また、作文でも役に立つ。 This grammar is often used in conversation. It's also useful in writing. この駅は便利だ。また、周りに店も多い。 This station is convenient. There are also a lot of shops nearby. さらに furthermore intensifying or extending a previous point stronger than simple “also”; often used in structured explanation 彼は日本語が話せる。さらに、中国語も話せる。 He can speak Japanese. Furthermore, he can speak Chinese as well. この薬は安い。さらに、効果も早い。 This medicine is inexpensive. Furthermore, it works quickly. そのうえ on top of that; moreover stacking an additional point with a stronger cumulative feel often emphasizes that the second point makes the overall case even stronger 彼は経験がある。そのうえ、教えるのも上手だ。 He has experience. On top of that, he's also good at teaching. このホテルは駅に近い。そのうえ、値段も安い。 This hotel is close to the station. On top of that, it's affordable. Result / conclusion だから so; therefore stating a direct conclusion from the previous sentence common in speech; straightforward and speaker-forward 今日は日曜日だ。だから、学校は休みだ。 Today is Sunday. So there's no school. 雨が降っている。だから、出かけない。 It's raining. So I'm not going out. それで and so; because of that showing that the second sentence follows as a result often sounds like natural narrative consequence 電車が遅れた。それで、会議に遅れた。 The train was delayed. So I was late for the meeting. 昨日はあまり寝なかった。それで、今日は眠い。 I didn't sleep much yesterday. Because of that, I'm sleepy today. そのため for that reason; therefore giving a more formal result or consequence common in written explanation, reports, and careful exposition 台風が近づいている。そのため、試合は中止になった。 A typhoon is approaching. For that reason, the game was canceled. 材料が不足している。そのため、生産が遅れている。 Materials are lacking. Therefore, production is delayed. したがって accordingly; therefore drawing a formal or logical conclusion more formal and deductive than だから この条件は満たされていない。したがって、申請は認められない。 These conditions are not satisfied. Accordingly, the application cannot be approved. 証拠が十分ではない。したがって、結論は保留する。 The evidence is not sufficient. Therefore, we will reserve judgment. Contrast / qualification でも but; however introducing an everyday contrast or objection very common in conversation; casual and direct 行きたい。でも、時間がない。 I want to go. But I don't have time. この店は高い。でも、おいしい。 This restaurant is expensive. But it's tasty. しかし however introducing a firmer contrast in neutral or formal style stronger and more written-sounding than でも 計画は良かった。しかし、実行が難しかった。 The plan was good. However, carrying it out was difficult. 彼は努力した。しかし、結果は出なかった。 He made an effort. However, it didn't pay off. ところが however; but then marking an unexpected turn against expectation often carries surprise: “but contrary to what you’d expect...” 簡単だと思った。ところが、全然できなかった。 I thought it would be easy. However, I couldn't do it at all. 天気予報では晴れだった。ところが、午後から雨が降った。 The forecast said it would be sunny. However, it started raining in the afternoon. ただし however; provided that adding a limitation, condition, or exception to what was just said common in instructions, rules, and formal explanation この図書館は誰でも使えます。ただし、貸し出しには登録が必要です。 Anyone can use this library. However, borrowing books requires registration. 参加は自由です。ただし、事前に連絡してください。 Participation is open. That said, please contact us in advance.
